questions.scenario:
A failed deploy shows:

`client spn-orders-release cannot perform Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries/pull/read over /subscriptions/sub-prod-001/resourceGroups/rg-platform-prod/providers/Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries/acrdevpolarisprod`

What should the team inspect first?

options.text: Whether the release identity has the needed ACR pull permission at the registry or relevant scope.
Whether the app has Key Vault access, because image pull failures usually come from missing secrets.
Whether the SQL database firewall is open, because container registries require database connectivity.
Whether the public DNS record points to the registry, because ACR pulls are controlled by custom domains.

questions.explanation: The denied action and scope point directly at Container Registry access. Key Vault, SQL, and public DNS can matter elsewhere, but not as the first interpretation of this error.

questions.scenario: An engineer can assign `Reader` but cannot assign `Contributor` at the same scope. What does that suggest? The engineer is using the same subscription and resource group in both attempts, so the difference appears to be the role being assigned.

options.text: Their permissions may allow limited role assignment or a constrained workflow, so the exact role assignment authority needs review.
Contributor is not a valid Azure role, so the portal blocks it for everyone.
Reader and Contributor are controlled by regions, so moving regions would fix it.
The engineer is signed in to the wrong browser profile, because role choice is stored locally.

questions.explanation: Role assignment permissions can be constrained. The exact allowed role definitions and scope matter. The behavior does not prove the role is invalid or regional.

questions.scenario: A production app receives `Forbidden` when writing blobs. The assigned role is `Storage Blob Data Reader` at the storage account scope. What is the best conclusion?

options.text: The identity has read-oriented data-plane access, but the write operation needs a different storage data role or scope.
The identity can write if the blob name includes `prod`, because names influence data-plane writes.
The app needs subscription Contributor, because all blob writes are management-plane operations.
The storage account should move to the app resource group, because data roles only work inside app groups.

questions.explanation: The role name itself is evidence: reader access is not writer access. Blob data operations are controlled by data-plane permissions and scopes, not by naming conventions.

questions.scenario: Which evidence should appear in a good RBAC incident note? The incident note will be handed to a security reviewer who was not on the call, so vague wording will not be enough.

options.text: The exact denied action from the error message.
The principal object ID or managed identity involved.
The scope where the action was attempted.
A broad statement that Azure permissions were broken.

questions.explanation: A useful incident note records concrete evidence: action, principal, and scope. A vague statement does not help a reviewer decide whether the fix was narrow and correct.

questions.scenario: A role assignment fix grants `Contributor` to the runtime managed identity so it can read one Key Vault secret. What should a reviewer say? The app already has a managed identity, and the requested job is a single secret read during startup.

options.text: The fix is too broad for the job. The app needs a secret-read path, not general resource modification power.
Contributor is the safest Key Vault role because it includes fewer secret actions than read-only roles.
Runtime identities should usually be subscription Contributors so app code can repair its own resources.
The role is fine if the app resource and Key Vault are in the same region.

questions.explanation: The requested job is reading a secret. Contributor grants management power that does not match the need. Region does not make a broad role safe.
